tcp-handshake.name: TCP 3-Way Handshake
tcp-handshake.description: The process by which TCP establishes a reliable connection between client and server
tcp-handshake.overview: Before any data can be exchanged over TCP, both the client and server must agree to communicate. The 3-way handshake synchronizes sequence numbers and establishes the connection parameters.

steps.label: SYN
steps.description: Client sends a SYN (synchronize) segment to the server with an initial sequence number (ISN). This is like raising your hand and saying 'I want to talk to you.'

steps.label: SYN-ACK
steps.description: Server responds with SYN-ACK — acknowledging the client's SYN and sending its own sequence number. This is the server saying 'I heard you and I want to talk too.'

steps.label: ACK
steps.description: Client sends an ACK confirming the server's sequence number. The connection is now established and data transfer can begin. Both sides are synchronized.

key_concepts: Sequence numbers are used to track and order data
Each side must acknowledge the other's sequence number
The handshake prevents old duplicate connections from being accepted
After the handshake, the connection is ESTABLISHED

tcp-retransmission.name: TCP Retransmission
tcp-retransmission.description: How TCP recovers from lost or corrupted packets by retransmitting them
tcp-retransmission.overview: TCP guarantees reliable delivery. When a sent packet doesn't receive an acknowledgment within a timeout period, TCP retransmits the data. This ensures no data is lost even on unreliable networks.

steps.label: Data Sent
steps.description: Client sends a data segment with sequence number. A retransmission timer starts.

steps.label: Packet Lost
steps.description: The packet is lost in transit due to network congestion, corruption, or hardware failure. The server never receives it.

steps.label: Timeout
steps.description: The retransmission timer expires (RTO). No ACK was received, so TCP assumes the packet was lost.

steps.label: Retransmit
steps.description: TCP retransmits the same data segment. The RTO is typically doubled (exponential backoff) for subsequent retransmissions.

steps.label: ACK Received
steps.description: Server receives the retransmitted packet and sends an ACK. Data delivery is confirmed and the sender can proceed.

key_concepts: RTO (Retransmission Timeout) is dynamically calculated based on RTT
Exponential backoff doubles the RTO after each failed retransmission
Fast Retransmit triggers after 3 duplicate ACKs without waiting for timeout
Selective ACK (SACK) allows acknowledging non-contiguous segments

tcp-congestion.name: TCP Congestion Control / Slow Start
tcp-congestion.description: How TCP manages sending rate to avoid overwhelming the network
tcp-congestion.overview: TCP doesn't blast data at full speed — it gradually increases its sending rate to probe available bandwidth. When congestion is detected, it backs off to prevent network collapse.

steps.label: Slow Start
steps.description: Connection begins with congestion window (cwnd) = 1 MSS. Each ACK doubles the window, causing exponential growth.

steps.label: Exponential Growth
steps.description: cwnd doubles each RTT: 1→2→4→8→16 segments. Bandwidth utilization rapidly increases.

steps.label: ssthresh Reached
steps.description: When cwnd reaches the slow-start threshold (ssthresh), growth switches from exponential to linear (congestion avoidance).

steps.label: Congestion Avoidance
steps.description: cwnd increases by 1 MSS per RTT — linear growth. This carefully probes for additional bandwidth.

steps.label: Congestion Detected
steps.description: Packet loss detected (timeout or 3 duplicate ACKs). ssthresh set to cwnd/2, cwnd resets. Recovery begins.

key_concepts: Slow Start rapidly finds available bandwidth
Congestion Avoidance carefully probes for more bandwidth
Packet loss signals congestion — TCP backs off
Modern TCP uses CUBIC or BBR algorithms for improved performance

udp-communication.name: UDP Communication
udp-communication.description: Connectionless, fast data transmission without reliability guarantees
udp-communication.overview: UDP is the 'fire and forget' protocol. It sends data without establishing a connection, without acknowledgments, and without guaranteeing delivery. This makes it faster but less reliable than TCP.

steps.label: Send Datagram
steps.description: Client sends a UDP datagram directly to the server. No connection setup needed — data is sent immediately.

steps.label: Received (maybe)
steps.description: If the datagram arrives, the server processes it. No acknowledgment is sent back. The client doesn't know if it arrived.

steps.label: Another Datagram
steps.description: Client sends another datagram. It may arrive before, after, or never. UDP doesn't guarantee ordering.

steps.label: Lost Datagram
steps.description: A datagram is lost in transit. UDP doesn't detect or recover from this — the application must handle it if needed.

key_concepts: No handshake — lower latency than TCP
No acknowledgments — sender doesn't know if data arrived
No ordering — packets may arrive out of order
Used for: DNS, VoIP, gaming, video streaming, DHCP

dns-lookup.name: DNS Lookup Process
dns-lookup.description: How domain names are resolved to IP addresses through the DNS hierarchy
dns-lookup.overview: When you type 'google.com' in your browser, a complex chain of queries converts that human-readable name into an IP address. This process involves multiple DNS servers in a hierarchical system.

steps.label: Browser Cache
steps.description: Browser first checks its local DNS cache. If the domain was recently resolved, the cached IP is used immediately.

steps.label: Recursive Resolver
steps.description: If not cached, the query goes to a recursive DNS resolver (usually your ISP's or a public one like 8.8.8.8).

steps.label: Root Server
steps.description: The resolver queries a root DNS server. It doesn't know google.com's IP, but it knows where to find .com servers.

steps.label: TLD Server
steps.description: The .com TLD server is queried. It refers the resolver to Google's authoritative nameserver.

steps.label: Authoritative Server
steps.description: Google's authoritative DNS server returns the actual IP address for google.com.

steps.label: Response
steps.description: The resolver caches the result and returns it to the browser. The browser can now connect to the IP address.

key_concepts: DNS is hierarchical: Root → TLD → Authoritative
Caching at every level reduces lookup time
TTL determines how long a record is cached
DNS typically uses UDP port 53 for queries

arp-resolution.name: ARP Resolution
arp-resolution.description: How devices discover MAC addresses on a local network using ARP
arp-resolution.overview: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) bridges the gap between IP addresses (Layer 3) and MAC addresses (Layer 2). When a device needs to send data on a local network, it must know the recipient's MAC address.

steps.label: Need MAC Address
steps.description: Device A wants to send data to 192.168.1.5 but doesn't know its MAC address. It checks its ARP cache first.

steps.label: ARP Request (Broadcast)
steps.description: Not in cache! Device A broadcasts an ARP request to all devices: 'Who has 192.168.1.5? Tell 192.168.1.1'

steps.label: ARP Reply (Unicast)
steps.description: Device B (192.168.1.5) responds with its MAC address: 'I am 192.168.1.5, my MAC is AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF'

steps.label: Cache Updated
steps.description: Device A stores the IP-to-MAC mapping in its ARP cache for future use (typically 2-20 minutes).

key_concepts: ARP operates at Layer 2 (Data Link) bridging to Layer 3 (Network)
ARP requests are broadcast, ARP replies are unicast
ARP cache entries expire after a timeout period
ARP spoofing is a common network attack vector

dhcp-assignment.name: DHCP IP Assignment
dhcp-assignment.description: How devices automatically obtain IP addresses using the DORA process
dhcp-assignment.overview: DHCP automates network configuration. Instead of manually setting IPs, devices can request all network settings from a DHCP server using the DORA process: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge.

steps.label: DHCP Discover
steps.description: New device broadcasts: 'Is there a DHCP server out there?' It has no IP yet, so it sends from 0.0.0.0 to 255.255.255.255.

steps.label: DHCP Offer
steps.description: DHCP server responds with an available IP address and configuration: 'You can have 192.168.1.100 for 24 hours.'

steps.label: DHCP Request
steps.description: Client broadcasts acceptance: 'I'll take that IP please!' (Broadcast so other DHCP servers know the offer was accepted elsewhere.)

steps.label: DHCP Acknowledge
steps.description: Server confirms and sends full configuration: IP, subnet mask, gateway, DNS servers, and lease duration.

key_concepts: DORA: Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge
Uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client)
Leases are temporary — clients must renew before expiry
DHCP also provides subnet mask, gateway, and DNS servers

http-vs-https.name: HTTP vs HTTPS Request Flow
http-vs-https.description: Comparing plaintext HTTP and encrypted HTTPS request flows
http-vs-https.overview: HTTP sends data in plaintext — anyone intercepting the traffic can read it. HTTPS adds TLS encryption, creating a secure tunnel. The extra TLS handshake adds latency but provides confidentiality and integrity.

steps.label: TCP Handshake
steps.description: Both HTTP and HTTPS start with the standard TCP 3-way handshake to establish a connection.

steps.label: TLS Handshake (HTTPS only)
steps.description: For HTTPS: Client and server negotiate encryption. Client verifies server's certificate. They agree on a shared encryption key.
steps.packet_label: 🔒 TLS: ClientHello → ServerHello → Certificate → Key Exchange

steps.label: HTTP Request
steps.description: HTTP: Request sent in PLAINTEXT — anyone can read it. HTTPS: Request is encrypted — only the server can decrypt it.

steps.label: Server Response
steps.description: Server sends back the response. HTTP: visible to eavesdroppers. HTTPS: encrypted end-to-end.
steps.packet_label: 200 OK + HTML — HTTP: readable 👁️ | HTTPS: encrypted 🔒

key_concepts: HTTPS = HTTP + TLS (Transport Layer Security)
TLS handshake adds 1-2 RTTs of latency
HTTPS verifies server identity via certificates
Modern HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 require HTTPS
